sections.heading: KEY REGULATORY INSTRUMENTS FOR THE ACTUALIZATION OF CLOUD
sections.title: Regulatory instruments for cloud computing implementation
sections.summary: This section identifies key regulatory instruments necessary for cloud computing implementation in Nigeria. It establishes the framework for cloud adoption by public institutions and SMEs, emphasizing the need for policy alignment with national digital strategies. The section outlines the role of NITDA in issuing cloud-related policies and the importance of cloud-first approaches for efficient IT resource deployment.
sections.dtia_summary: This section sets the foundation for cloud computing adoption by public institutions and SMEs in Nigeria, requiring alignment with national digital strategies and regulatory frameworks. It establishes the 'cloud-first' approach as a key policy thrust for efficient IT resource deployment.

requirements.text: Key regulatory instruments for cloud computing implementation must be established

sections.source_quote: KEY REGULATORY INSTRUMENTS FOR THE ACTUALIZATION OF CLOUD 23.0

sections.title: Cloud computing policy implementation framework
sections.summary: This section details the implementation framework for cloud computing in Nigeria, specifying that the policy applies to federal public institutions, state and local government entities, and government-owned corporations. It mandates that public sector entities prioritize cloud-based procurement of ICT solutions to reduce costs, improve service delivery, and enhance digital transformation. The policy targets a 30% increase in cloud adoption by 2024 among public institutions and SMEs providing digital services to government.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates that public sector entities prioritize cloud-based procurement of ICT solutions, requiring data transfers to cloud environments to be assessed for compliance with national security and data governance standards.

requirements.text: The policy applies to all Federal Public Institutions, Public Institutions at State and Local Government levels, and government-owned corporations
Public sector entities shall prioritize procurement of cloud-based ICT solutions whenever possible
The policy targets a 30% increase in cloud adoption by 2024 among Federal Public Institutions and SMEs providing digital services to government
The policy targets 35% growth in cloud computing investments by 2024

sections.source_quote: The Nigeria Cloud Computing Policy is issued pursuant to Section 6 (a) (b) (c) and (i) of the National Information Technology Development Act 2007.

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Cloud Computing Service Models and Deployment Options
sections.summary: This section describes cloud computing service models (SaaS, PaaS, IaaS) and deployment models (private, public, hybrid clouds). It outlines how public institutions in Nigeria can leverage these models for efficient service delivery, cost savings, and resource optimization without managing underlying infrastructure. The section emphasizes transitioning from traditional IT systems to cloud solutions to improve responsiveness, transparency, and resource allocation while avoiding vendor lock-in.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines cloud service models and deployment options critical for DTIA as it specifies how data storage and processing must align with Nigerian territorial boundaries and security requirements for different data sensitivity levels. It mandates that public institutions assess cloud solutions based on data classification to ensure compliance with national security and data protection standards.

requirements.text: Public Institutions will leverage cloud services to provide improved responsiveness to citizens' needs and increased transparency in healthcare, social amenities, justice, public safety, and education services.
Cloud technologies will create competitive advantages for SMEs by generating employment opportunities, developing indigenous technology, and diversifying the economy.
Migrating to cloud can streamline processes by consolidating dispersed IT systems under a central government platform, reducing costs through 'as needed' resource procurement.
Public Institutions must use cloud services to move routine IT tasks to providers, allowing staff to focus on value-adding skills like application development.
Cloud service providers must offer infrastructure that allows provisioning of processing, storage, networks, and other computing resources without managing underlying infrastructure.
Cloud service providers must provide security certifications assessed by third-party professionals to ensure data security.

sections.source_quote: Cloud computing allows for new features to be continuously deployed, while the costs are amortised across a global service customer base.

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Cross-Border Data Flows and Data Classification for Cloud Services
sections.summary: This section addresses cross-border data flows in cloud environments, requiring public institutions to ensure cloud service providers store data in jurisdictions with equivalent data protection standards to Nigeria. It mandates data classification based on sensitivity levels (public, confidential, secret, classified) to determine appropriate cloud storage locations and security measures, with classified data restricted to Nigerian territory or private cloud solutions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is critical for DTIA as it specifies jurisdictional requirements for cross-border data flows and mandates data classification to ensure compliance with Nigerian territorial boundaries and security standards for different data sensitivity levels.

requirements.text: Public Institutions must contract cloud service providers that store data in jurisdictions with equivalent personal data protection standards to Nigeria.
Data classified as 'secret' must reside primarily within Nigerian territorial boundaries with encryption and strong user authentication.
Data classified as 'classified' must remain within Nigerian territory or collocated in Nigerian cloud environments.
Public Institutions must classify data into four sensitivity levels to determine appropriate cloud storage and security measures.
Cloud service providers must provide security certifications assessed by third-party professionals to ensure data security.

sections.source_quote: Agencies shall consider focusing on access to data when required. To this end, NITDA will ensure that CSPs provide adequate security and privacy measures and transparency around data compliance.

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Cloud service contracts for government entities require minimum data protection clauses
sections.summary: This section mandates that NITDA develops a regulatory framework for cloud contracts between public institutions and cloud service providers (CSPs). The framework ensures government entities receive equivalent rights to private customers and includes minimum contractual requirements such as CSP due diligence compliance, data handling procedures, data retrieval rights upon termination, liability limitations, and security certifications. These provisions establish baseline contractual obligations for data protection in government cloud deployments.
sections.dtia_summary: Government cloud contracts must include specific data protection clauses like data retrieval rights upon termination and liability limitations, which directly impact cross-border data transfer safeguards when government entities use international CSPs.

requirements.text: NITDA will work on the development of a regulatory framework for the execution of cloud computing contracts between Public Institutions and Cloud Service Providers (CSP)
Government entities using the cloud as cloud customers enjoy at least the same rights as those enjoyed by individual customers, enterprises and other cloud customers
CSP adherence to the due diligence process and conformity of public procurement guidelines/processes
a description of services to be provided
the contract's duration (unless it is of unlimited duration)
payment terms and termination
details on the available Service Level Agreements (SLA)
rules on handling cloud customer data, including their processing, destruction and restoration
CSP's customer care services depending on a particular service offering
customers' right to retrieve their data stored in the CSP's system, if the cloud contract is terminated

limitation of CSPs' right to exclude their liability unreasonably or to impose unfair contract terms related to loss of, or damage to, customer's data, quality of service degradations such as service unavailability, or data breaches
Level of cloud security and privacy

sections.source_quote: NITDA will work on the development of a regulatory framework for the execution of cloud computing contracts between Public Institutions and Cloud Service Providers (CSP). This regulatory framework shall ensure that government entities using the cloud as cloud customers enjoy at least the same rights as those enjoyed by individual customers, enterprises and other cloud customers.

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Cloud governance framework for public sector cloud adoption
sections.summary: This section establishes a four-tiered cloud governance framework for Nigerian public sector entities, requiring coordination between NITDA, Bureau of Public Procurement, and other agencies. It mandates specific governance responsibilities including cloud readiness assessments, procurement regulation development, security monitoring, and standardization with international bodies like NIST and ISO. The framework ensures accountability across infrastructure, operating systems, applications, and user activities while defining roles for cloud adoption and migration.
sections.dtia_summary: Public sector cloud governance requires specific oversight mechanisms for data protection and service continuity, directly impacting cross-border data transfers through contractual obligations and security standards.

requirements.text: The plan shall operate across four levels of cloud governance: infrastructure, operating system, platform or application, business/user activity
The following bodies shall have these roles and responsibilities: Bureau of Public Procurement shall develop and operationalize government-wide procurement regulation for Cloud services
NITDA will liaise with the BPP to accommodate cloud procurement models in the procurement process
NITDA shall conduct a Cloud readiness assessment
The Office for National Security Adviser (ONSA) and NITDA shall monitor operational security issues related to the cloud
NITDA shall drive government-wide adoption of cloud, identify next-generation cloud technologies, share best practices, templates and reusable example
NITDA shall coordinate activities across governance bodies, set overall cloud-related priorities, and provide guidance to agencies
NITDA shall monitor, identify and prioritize cloud computing standards and guidance from NIST, ISO and other relevant international standards organisations

sections.source_quote: The Bureau of Public Procurement shall develop and operationalize government-wide procurement regulation for Cloud services in consultation with NITDA and other relevant agencies of government
